title: Deleting Folders
subtitle: Remove directories with rm -r

sections.content: Deleting a folder is a bit different from deleting a file. You need to tell `rm` to go **recursive** — meaning delete everything inside, then the folder itself.
sections.analogy: You can't demolish a room that still has furniture in it. `rm -r` clears everything out first, then removes the room.
keyPoints: `rm -r` deletes a folder and everything inside it
The `-r` flag means **recursive** — it goes through all contents
Be very careful — this deletes everything with no undo

sections.instruction: Deleting folders is slightly different — you need to tell `rm` to go 'recursive'.
sections.prompt: Try deleting `old-stuff` the same way as a file: `rm old-stuff`.

onSuccess.message: You got an error: regular `rm` only works on files. That's by design — it prevents accidental folder deletion.

sections.instruction: To delete a folder and everything inside it, add `-r` (recursive).

onSuccess.message: The folder and all its contents are gone. `-r` means 'go through everything inside, then delete the folder too'.

sections.question: Why does `rm myfolder` fail without the `-r` flag?
options: You need to empty the folder first
`rm` only deletes files — folders require `-r` (recursive) to delete their contents too
The folder name needs quotes around it
sections.explanation: A folder can contain files and other folders inside it. The `-r` flag tells `rm` to recursively go inside and delete everything, then remove the folder itself.

completionMessage: `rm -r` deletes a folder and EVERYTHING inside it. Always double-check the folder name before pressing Enter.
